title: Topic 264: Why Licensed Games Are Usually Bad: The Psychology of Cash Grabs
content:
> **Topic 264: Why Licensed Games Are Usually Bad: The Psychology of Cash Grabs**

**ABSTRACT**

This paper bravely ventures into the treacherous, pixelated landscape of licensed video games, a realm where beloved characters often go to have their digital souls crushed. We explore the age-old question: why are these games, born from the loins of blockbuster movies and cherished comics, so frequently… well, terrible? This study dissects the anatomy of the "cash grab," a phenomenon driven by a potent cocktail of cynical marketing and consumer psychology. We delve into the psychological underpinnings that make us susceptible to these digital disappointments, from the intoxicating allure of brand loyalty to the mental gymnastics of cognitive dissonance. While the history of licensed games is littered with the corpses of shattered expectations, this paper offers a glimmer of hope, a post-mortem on the psychology of our collective, and often inexplicable, purchasing decisions. In the end, we are left to ponder if the true final boss is not a poorly rendered villain, but our own unwavering optimism.

**INTRODUCTION**

It is a truth universally acknowledged that a gamer in possession of a fond childhood memory must be in want of a licensed video game. And so it was, in the halcyon days of the Atari 2600, that a generation of wide-eyed children, their hearts full of cinematic wonder, first encountered the crushing reality of the licensed game. The year was 1982, and the game was *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial*. Fresh from the tear-jerking embrace of Steven Spielberg’s masterpiece, young gamers eagerly inserted the cartridge, only to find themselves in a digital purgatory of falling into pits and endlessly extending an alien’s neck. This, dear reader, was for many the first, bitter taste of the “cash grab.”

This paper will explore the fascinating and often frustrating world of licensed video games, and the psychological machinations that make them such a reliable source of disappointment. We define a “licensed game” as a video game based on a pre-existing intellectual property (IP) from another medium, such as a film, television show, or comic book. A “cash grab,” in this context, refers to a product created with the primary goal of capitalizing on the popularity of the licensed IP, often with minimal regard for quality, originality, or player experience. Our central thesis is that the consistent failure of licensed games is not merely a matter of lazy development, but a complex interplay of economic pressures, cynical marketing, and predictable patterns of human psychology.

We will embark on a historical journey, from the infamous Atari video game burial to the modern era of mobile shovelware, to understand the evolution of this phenomenon. We will then delve into the psychological theories that explain why we, as consumers, continue to fall for these digital sirens’ songs. Finally, we will analyze the anatomy of the cash grab from both the developer’s and the consumer’s perspective, using real-world examples to illustrate our points. By the end of this paper, you may not feel any better about that time you spent your allowance on the *Superman 64* cartridge, but you will, at the very least, understand *why* you did it.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not dedicated entire libraries to the study of why the *Catwoman* video game was an affront to feline agility and basic game design. However, the underlying psychological principles that explain our baffling attraction to such digital refuse are well-documented. The journey into the consumer’s mind begins with the concept of **moral licensing**, or self-licensing. This theory posits that individuals, after performing a moral or “good” act, are more likely to engage in subsequent immoral or “bad” behavior [1]. In the context of licensed games, our past positive experiences with a brand—binge-watching every episode of a beloved TV show, for instance—acts as a form of “moral currency.” We have been “good” fans, and thus, we subconsciously grant ourselves a license to make a questionable purchase. The game doesn’t have to be good; our loyalty has already paid for it.

This leads us to the thorny embrace of **cognitive dissonance**, the psychological discomfort we feel when our beliefs and actions are in conflict [2]. When we, as devoted fans, purchase a game that turns out to be a steaming pile of digital garbage, we are faced with a choice: either admit that we have been duped, or perform a series of mental gymnastics to justify our purchase. “It’s not *that* bad,” we might tell ourselves, “and besides, I’m supporting the franchise!” This is the halo effect in action, where our positive feelings for the source material cast a deceptive glow on the demonstrably subpar game. As one article on the history of licensed games notes, the problem is often that developers are given an impossibly short amount of time to create a game that can live up to the hype of the source material [3].

Furthermore, the very nature of video games as a medium makes them fertile ground for these psychological phenomena. Video games, as a form of entertainment, are designed to be rewarding. They tap into our innate desire for achievement and mastery [4]. When a game is attached to a beloved franchise, it promises to extend that experience, to allow us to step into the shoes of our heroes. This potent combination of brand loyalty and the inherent appeal of the medium creates a perfect storm of consumer vulnerability. The “cash grab” is not just a cynical business practice; it is a carefully orchestrated exploitation of our most basic psychological drives.

**ANALYSIS**

To truly understand the persistence of the licensed game as a cultural punching bag, we must dissect the anatomy of the “cash grab” from both sides of the transaction. It is a symbiotic relationship, a dance of cynical opportunism and hopeful naivete, where both developer and consumer play their parts to perfection.

From the perspective of the publisher and developer, the licensed game represents a tantalizingly low-risk, high-reward proposition. The marketing is, for the most part, already done. The intellectual property comes with a built-in audience, a legion of fans already emotionally and financially invested in the world and its characters. This pre-existing brand awareness significantly lowers the barrier to entry, as the game doesn’t need to build a following from scratch. It can simply ride the coattails of the summer blockbuster or the hit TV show. This is particularly true for games aimed at younger audiences, who are more susceptible to the allure of their favorite characters on a game box [3].

This built-in audience also allows for a more… *relaxed* approach to development. Why spend years crafting a masterpiece when a passable imitation will sell just as well, if not better? The development cycle for licensed games is often brutally short, designed to coincide with the release of the source material. As was the case with *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial*, developers can be given a matter of weeks to produce a game, a task that would be daunting even for the most seasoned of teams [3]. The result is often a rushed, buggy, and ultimately unsatisfying experience. The goal is not to create a lasting work of art, but to get a product on the shelves before the cultural zeitgeist moves on.

On the other side of the screen is the consumer, the hopeful fan who, against all odds, continues to believe that *this time* it will be different. The psychology of the licensed game consumer is a fascinating study in optimism and self-deception. The primary driver, of course, is the power of nostalgia and emotional connection. We don’t just want to play a game; we want to relive the magic of the movie, to become the hero we admired on the screen. This desire to extend the narrative experience is a powerful motivator, one that can easily override our better judgment.

This is where the “fear of missing out” (FOMO) comes into play. In an increasingly interconnected world, being a fan is a participatory activity. We want to be part of the conversation, to share in the collective experience. A new licensed game, no matter how dubious its quality, is a new piece of the franchise’s universe, a new topic of discussion. To not play it is to risk being left out, to be a less “authentic” fan. This social pressure, combined with the sunk cost fallacy of our emotional investment in the franchise, makes us surprisingly willing to part with our hard-earned cash for a product that, by all objective measures, is simply not very good.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

To illustrate the theoretical framework outlined above, let us now turn our attention to the digital battlefield, where the casualties of the licensed game wars are legion. These are not mere cautionary tales; they are rites of passage for a generation of gamers, formative experiences that taught us the cruel sting of disappointment.

**Case Study 1: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial (Atari 2600)**

No discussion of bad licensed games would be complete without a pilgrimage to the holy land of digital despair: the Atari 2600’s adaptation of *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial*. Rushed to market in a mere five and a half weeks to capitalize on the film’s success, the game was a commercial and critical disaster of such epic proportions that it is often cited as a major contributor to the video game crash of 1983 [3]. The gameplay, a bewildering exercise in falling into pits and searching for pieces of a telephone, bore little resemblance to the heartwarming tale of friendship and wonder that had captivated audiences. The game’s failure was so absolute that unsold cartridges were famously buried in a New Mexico landfill, a fittingly ignominious end for a game that had so thoroughly betrayed the trust of its players.

**Case Study 2: Superman 64**

Fast forward to the 64-bit era, and we find another contender for the title of “worst licensed game of all time”: *Superman 64*. Here was a game that took the Man of Steel, a character defined by his incredible powers and unwavering commitment to justice, and reduced him to a glorified errand boy, forced to fly through a series of rings in a perpetually foggy Metropolis. The game’s frustrating controls, repetitive missions, and complete failure to capture the essence of its source material made it a legendary disappointment. It stands as a monument to the hubris of developers who believe that a famous license is a substitute for good game design.

**Case Study 3: The Modern Shovelware Epidemic**

The advent of mobile gaming has ushered in a new golden age of the cash grab. App stores are flooded with low-quality, ad-riddled games featuring popular characters from children’s television shows and movies. These games, often developed with minimal effort and designed to exploit the “pester power” of children, are the modern-day equivalent of the Atari-era shovelware. They are a testament to the enduring appeal of the cash grab, a business model that continues to thrive in the digital age.

**A Glimmer of Hope: The Batman: Arkham Series**

Lest we descend into complete and utter despair, it is worth noting that not all licensed games are created equal. The *Batman: Arkham* series stands as a shining example of what can be achieved when a talented developer is given the time, resources, and creative freedom to do justice to a beloved IP. These games not only captured the dark and gritty atmosphere of the Batman universe but also introduced innovative gameplay mechanics that have since been imitated by countless other titles. The *Arkham* series proves that a licensed game can be more than just a cynical cash grab; it can be a work of art in its own right.

**DISCUSSION**

The phenomenon of the bad licensed game, while endlessly amusing to dissect, has broader implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. It is a stark reminder that we are not always the rational, discerning consumers we like to think we are. Our purchasing decisions are often driven by emotion, nostalgia, and a deep-seated desire to connect with the stories and characters that have shaped our lives. The cash grab is not just a gaming problem; it is a human problem.

The same psychological principles that make us susceptible to bad licensed games are at play in countless other areas of consumer culture. From overpriced movie merchandise to celebrity-endorsed fashion lines, the cash grab is a ubiquitous feature of the modern marketplace. It is a business model built on the exploitation of our emotional attachments, a cynical bet that our love for a brand will outweigh our desire for quality.

This raises important ethical questions, particularly when it comes to marketing these products to children. Are we, as a society, comfortable with a business model that so blatantly preys on the innocence and enthusiasm of young fans? The long-term effects on brand reputation are also worth considering. While a successful cash grab can provide a short-term financial boost, it can also erode the trust and goodwill that a franchise has built up over years, or even decades. In the end, the greatest casualty of the cash grab may not be our wallets, but our faith in the brands we love.

**CONCLUSION**

In the grand, pixelated tapestry of video game history, the bad licensed game holds a special, if somewhat dubious, place of honor. It is a testament to the enduring power of hope over experience, a monument to our collective willingness to believe that *this time*, it will be different. We have journeyed through the psychological landscape of the cash grab, from the heady heights of brand loyalty to the murky depths of cognitive dissonance. We have seen how a combination of cynical marketing and our own psychological foibles can lead us to make some truly questionable purchasing decisions.

And yet, for all our cynicism, for all our battle scars, we remain a hopeful bunch. We continue to line up for the midnight releases, to pre-order the special editions, to believe that the next licensed game will be the one that finally gets it right. Perhaps this is the ultimate irony of the cash grab: it is a business model that depends on our disappointment, but it is also a business model that is sustained by our eternal, unyielding optimism. The game may be bad, but the dream, dear reader, the dream is still very much alive.
references:
[1] Merritt, A. C., Effron, D. A., & Monin, B. (2010). Moral self-licensing: When being good frees us to be bad. *Social and Personality Psychology Compass*, 4(5), 344-357.

[2] Festinger, L. (1957). *A theory of cognitive dissonance*. Stanford university press.

[3] Cornelius, J. (2014, March 27). A History of Vileness: The Problem With Licensed Video Games. *Bleeding Cool*. Retrieved from https://bleedingcool.com/comics/recent-updates/a-history-of-vileness-the-problem-with-licensed-video-games/

[4] Ryan, R. M., Rigby, C. S., & Przybylski, A. (2006). The motivational pull of video games: A self-determination theory approach. *Motivation and emotion*, 30(4), 344-360.
